title: Common Verbs
description: Learn 16 essential Vietnamese verbs that form the backbone of everyday conversation — and discover why Vietnamese verbs are simpler than you think.

sections.content:
Here's the best news about Vietnamese grammar: verbs NEVER conjugate. There are no verb endings, no irregular forms, no tense changes. The verb stays the same no matter who does it or when it happened.

Compare:
• English: I eat, he eats, I ate, I have eaten, I will eat
• Vietnamese: ăn, ăn, ăn, ăn, ăn

The verb 'ăn' never changes. Instead, Vietnamese uses time words to show when something happens:
• Hôm qua tôi ăn phở. (Yesterday I eat pho.) = I ate pho yesterday.
• Ngày mai tôi ăn phở. (Tomorrow I eat pho.) = I will eat pho tomorrow.
• Tôi đang ăn phở. ('đang' = currently) = I am eating pho.

As a heritage speaker, you've been doing this naturally your whole life — now you know why it works.

items.notes: One of the first words any Vietnamese person learns. 'Ăn cơm' = eat rice (but really means 'eat a meal'). 'Ăn sáng' = eat breakfast.

exampleSentence.english: Have you eaten yet? (very common greeting)

items.notes: 'Uống nước' = drink water. 'Uống cà phê' = drink coffee. 'Uống thuốc' = take medicine (literally 'drink medicine').

exampleSentence.english: Drink some water, it's very hot.

items.notes: 'Đi ngủ' = go to sleep. 'Buồn ngủ' = sleepy. 'Ngủ trưa' = take a nap (afternoon sleep — very common in Vietnam).

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Khuya rồi, đi ngủ đi con!

items.notes: 'Đi' is one of the most versatile words in Vietnamese. It means 'go' but also acts as a command particle: 'ăn đi!' = eat! (go ahead and eat). 'Đi chơi' = go out/hang out.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Cuối tuần mình đi chơi nha.
exampleSentence.english: Let's go hang out this weekend.

items.notes: 'Đến' indicates movement toward a destination. 'Đến rồi' = already arrived. Can also mean 'until' in time expressions.
'Về nhà' = go home. 'Về' implies returning to where you belong, especially home. 'Về Việt Nam' = go back to Vietnam.

exampleSentence.english: What time is dad coming home?

items.notes: 'Nói tiếng Việt' = speak Vietnamese. 'Nói chuyện' = talk/chat. Heritage speakers often hear 'nói tiếng Việt đi!' from their parents.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Nói tiếng Việt đi con, đừng nói tiếng Anh!

items.notes: Southern Vietnamese uses 'nghe' where Northern uses 'nghe thấy' for 'to hear'. 'Nghe nè' = listen here (Southern, informal).

exampleSentence.english: Listen to what mom is saying!

items.notes: 'Nhìn' = to look (active). 'Thấy' = to see (perception). 'Nhìn thấy' = to catch sight of. In Southern speech, 'thấy' is extremely common as a general 'see/perceive'.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Con có thấy chìa khóa của ba không?

items.notes: 'Biết' = know a fact or how to do something. 'Biết nấu ăn' = know how to cook. 'Không biết' = don't know. Different from 'quen biết' (to be acquainted with someone).

exampleSentence.english: Do you know how to cook rice?

items.notes: 'Hiểu không?' = Do you understand? 'Không hiểu' = don't understand. Heritage speakers often understand more than they can speak — 'nghe hiểu nhưng không nói được'.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Con hiểu không? Hiểu rồi thì làm đi.
exampleSentence.english: Do you understand? If you understand, then do it.

items.english: to do / to make / to work
items.notes: Extremely versatile. 'Làm gì?' = what are you doing? 'Làm việc' = to work. 'Làm ơn' = please (literally 'do a favor'). 'Làm bài' = do homework.
'Muốn' expresses desire. 'Muốn ăn gì?' = What do you want to eat? Politely, you might say 'Con muốn xin...' (I would like to ask for...).

exampleSentence.english: What do you want to eat?

items.notes: 'Có' is one of the most important words in Vietnamese. It means 'to have', 'there is/are', and also marks yes/no questions: 'Có...không?' = Is there...? 'Có' alone = yes (to a 'có...không' question).

exampleSentence.english: Is there anything to eat at home?

items.notes: 'Cho' has many uses: give (cho con tiền = give the child money), let/allow (cho con đi = let me go), for (mua cho con = buy for the child). Context is everything.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Má cho con tiền đi mua đồ.
exampleSentence.english: Mom, give me money to go buy things.

items.english: to take / to get / to grab
items.notes: 'Lấy' = take/grab/get. 'Lấy nước' = get water. 'Lấy vợ/chồng' = get married (literally 'take a wife/husband'). Very common in daily Southern speech.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Lấy cho ba ly nước đi con.
exampleSentence.english: Get dad a glass of water.

sections.content: 'Con ăn cơm chưa?' (Have you eaten yet?) is one of the most common Vietnamese greetings — it's the equivalent of 'How are you?' You don't need to answer with what you ate; a simple 'Dạ, ăn rồi' (Yes, I've eaten) or 'Chưa' (Not yet) is fine. This greeting reflects Vietnamese culture's deep connection between food, care, and family.

Quick guide to Vietnamese time markers (since verbs don't change):

• đã / rồi = already happened (past): 'Tôi đã ăn rồi' = I already ate
• đang = happening now (present): 'Tôi đang ăn' = I am eating
• sẽ = will happen (future): 'Tôi sẽ ăn' = I will eat
• chưa = not yet: 'Tôi chưa ăn' = I haven't eaten yet

In casual Southern speech, 'đã' is often dropped — people just use 'rồi' at the end: 'Ăn rồi' = Already ate.

exercises.question: Why does the Vietnamese verb 'ăn' stay the same in 'Hôm qua tôi ăn phở' and 'Ngày mai tôi ăn phở'?
options: Vietnamese verbs never conjugate — time words show tense
exercises.correctAnswer: Vietnamese verbs never conjugate — time words show tense
exercises.explanation: Vietnamese verbs NEVER change form. Instead, time words like 'hôm qua' (yesterday), 'đang' (currently), and 'sẽ' (will) indicate when something happens.

exercises.question: What does 'Con ăn cơm chưa?' mean, and when would someone say it?
options: 'What are you cooking?' — asked when planning dinner
'Have you eaten yet?' — a common greeting
'Do you want to eat rice?' — an invitation to dinner
'Are you hungry?' — asked when someone looks tired
exercises.correctAnswer: 'Have you eaten yet?' — a common greeting
exercises.explanation: 'Con ăn cơm chưa?' literally means 'Have you eaten rice yet?' and is one of the most common Vietnamese greetings, equivalent to 'How are you?'

exercises.question: Your parent asks: 'Con đang ___ gì vậy?' (What are you doing?). Type the missing verb:
exercises.explanation: 'Làm' means 'to do' or 'to make'. 'Con đang làm gì vậy?' = 'What are you doing?' — a phrase every Vietnamese kid has heard a thousand times.

exercises.question: Which word marks that something is happening RIGHT NOW?
exercises.explanation: 'Đang' indicates a current, ongoing action (like English '-ing'). 'Tôi đang ăn' = I am eating (right now).

exercises.question: How do you say 'I don't understand' in Vietnamese? Type just the two words:
exercises.explanation: 'Không hiểu' = don't understand. 'Không' negates any verb. Heritage speakers know this phrase well — it's what you say when relatives speak too fast!
